title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of E-Sports on Traditional Athletics

texts.A: The inclusion of e-sports in multi-sport events is a desperate attempt to stay relevant in a digital age, but it fundamentally misinterprets what 'sport' is. Athletics is defined by the mastery of the human body and the physical limits of endurance. [37]Moving a thumb on a controller, regardless of the 'reflexes' required, does not belong in the same category as a marathon or a swim meet.[/37] [40]By equating sedentary gaming with physical exertion, we are sending a dangerous message to a generation already facing an obesity crisis.[/40] We should celebrate gaming as a hobby, but never as a sport.
texts.B: [39]The definition of sport has always been fluid, evolving alongside technology.[/39] [37]Those who cling to 'physicality' as the sole metric are ignoring the intense mental stamina, strategic depth, and hand-eye coordination required at the elite level of gaming.[/37] E-sports athletes undergo rigorous training regimes, often including physical fitness to improve their cognitive performance. The commercial success of e-sports is not a threat to traditional athletics; rather, it is a testament to a new form of human excellence that combines digital literacy with competitive drive.
texts.C: [39]I agree that the 'physicality' argument is increasingly outdated in a world where chess and bridge are recognized as sports by many Olympic committees.[/39] However, [38]the real issue with e-sports is the ownership of the 'field.' In soccer or running, no single corporation owns the rules or the ball. In gaming, the entire ecosystem is controlled by a private company that can change the physics of the world at any moment for profit.[/38] This lack of transparency and public 'ownership' is what truly separates competitive gaming from the democratic tradition of global athletics.
texts.D: The debate over whether gaming is a sport is a distraction from the real value e-sports bring to the table: inclusivity. Traditional sports often exclude those with physical disabilities or those who lack the resources for expensive equipment and facilities. E-sports provides a global, accessible platform where the only barrier to entry is a screen and an internet connection. [38]While I share the concern that the industry is dominated by corporate interests, I believe this is a logistical hurdle that can be solved with better regulation.[/38] [40]The cultural impact of e-sports is overwhelmingly positive.[/40]

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the validity of e-sports as a competitive discipline?
questions.explanation: Expert A believes gaming does not belong in the same category as sports. Expert B argues that physicality is a narrow metric and that e-sports represent a 'new form of human excellence.'

questions.question: Which expert shares C's concern regarding the corporate control of e-sports?
questions.explanation: Expert C mentions the 'ownership of the field' by private companies. Expert D also acknowledges that the industry is 'dominated by corporate interests.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to B about the evolving definition of what constitutes a sport?
questions.explanation: Expert B says the definition is 'fluid.' Expert C agrees, noting that the 'physicality argument is increasingly outdated' and pointing to chess as an example.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from D on the social and health impact of promoting e-sports?
questions.explanation: Expert D sees the impact as 'overwhelmingly positive' due to inclusivity. Expert A sees it as 'dangerous' because it promotes a sedentary lifestyle during a health crisis.
